content.text: Every HTTP request your agent issues can fail. Network blips, overloaded upstreams, and invalid inputs all produce errors — and the category of failure determines the correct response.
is the practice of inspecting each failure, classifying it, and taking the right action: raise immediately on a caller mistake, or back off and retry on a transient server fault.
Retries with exponential backoff and jitter

are the primary recovery tool — but only when applied to the correct error class. This topic covers HTTP error taxonomy, backoff mathematics, idempotency safety, structured error parsing, the tenacity library, and the circuit breaker pattern.
means reading the HTTP status code and response body from every call, deciding which failure category it belongs to, and taking the action that category requires.
means re-issuing the same request after a computed delay when the failure is transient. Together they form the resilience layer that sits between your application logic and the unreliable network.
HTTP status codes encode the failure category. The
family signals the server understood and deliberately rejected the request — the caller made a mistake. The
family signals the server failed internally, which is typically transient. Treating both uniformly — retrying everything, or retrying nothing — is the most common source of brittle API clients.

A single API call passes through load balancers, reverse proxies, application servers, and databases before a response arrives. Any layer can fail momentarily. Without a retry strategy, a 50 ms network blip causes a user-visible failure that requires manual intervention. With correct retry logic, the same blip is invisible. Conversely, retrying a
wastes time — the server will return the same error every time because the request itself is wrong. Retrying a
that already succeeded can create duplicate records.

For agentic AI workflows, error handling is especially critical: an agent may chain dozens of tool calls across minutes of work. A single unhandled transient error without a retry aborts an expensive pipeline that is otherwise correct. Precise classification and exponential backoff let agents heal from transient faults automatically while surfacing permanent errors immediately so the agent can report them rather than spinning indefinitely.
— transient server faults recover automatically without user intervention
— 4xx errors are surfaced immediately with actionable messages, not wasted on futile retries
— only safe-to-repeat operations are retried, preventing duplicate writes or double charges
— exponential backoff with jitter prevents thundering-herd retry storms that further overload a struggling server
— structured error bodies carry request IDs and field-level details that operators and agents can act on directly
— retries add wall-clock time before a final failure is reported; user-facing flows may time out before recovery
— enabling retries on writes requires server endpoints that are idempotent or accept idempotency keys — a design constraint
— clients retrying simultaneously on a partial outage worsen it if jitter is omitted
— a correct retry wrapper handling jitter, caps, idempotency, circuit breaking, and structured error parsing is non-trivial to write from scratch
The retry loop follows a four-step cycle:
the error as permanent (4xx) or transient (5xx/network), then
on transient errors using exponential backoff, or raise immediately on permanent errors. Each layer has specific rules.
HTTP status codes group into five classes.
informational codes are rarely seen by application code.
success codes mean the operation completed — no retry needed.
redirect codes should be followed automatically by the HTTP client.
client error codes mean the caller sent a bad request — retrying the same request will not help.
server error codes mean the server failed internally — the request was valid, and retrying after a delay is often successful.
Validation failed on one or more fields
Too many requests; honour Retry-After header
The decision of whether to retry must be made at a single classification layer, not duplicated across call sites.
errors are those where the same request could succeed if re-sent after a short wait:
if present; otherwise back off exponentially
— gateway or upstream infrastructure failure; always retryable
— network-level failures before a response is received; retryable for idempotent methods
— sometimes transient (e.g. during a rolling deployment); retryable for idempotent requests
Non-retryable errors are those where repeating the request produces the same result because the fault is in the request itself:
— malformed JSON or invalid query parameters; fix the client before retrying
— API key missing or invalid; rotate credentials before any retry
— permission denied; retrying with the same credentials always fails
— resource missing or body fails validation; retrying without fixing the request is futile
seconds. After attempt 0 the wait is
(e.g. 1 s); after attempt 1 it doubles to 2 s; after attempt 2 to 4 s, and so on up to
(e.g. 30 s). This gives the server increasing time to recover between probes.
Without jitter, all clients that hit the same transient error simultaneously retry at exactly the same moment — the
). This spreads retries across the backoff window and reduces the peak retry rate to approximately 1/N of the original spike for N concurrent clients.
Different vendors use different error body shapes. Anthropic and Stripe nest the message under
; GitHub and many simple APIs use
at the top level; FastAPI uses
. Parse gracefully by probing a priority list of paths, falling back to the raw text if all fail:

attrs.alt: API error handling flow: HTTP Response - Status Code Check - Error Taxonomy (4xx/5xx/Network) - Retry Decision - Backoff Strategy

content.text: The diagram shows the three-branch decision tree. An HTTP response is received and its status is classified. The
branch (left, red) raises a client error immediately — retrying is wasteful since the server's answer is deterministic for the same request. The
branch (centre, amber) and the network error branch (right, blue) both feed into the exponential backoff + jitter retry loop at the bottom, capped at five attempts.
Any code calling an external HTTP API — third-party services, internal microservices, or LLM provider APIs
Agentic pipelines where a failed tool call would abort a multi-step workflow that took significant time to reach
Background jobs and data ingestion pipelines that cannot prompt a user to retry manually
GET-heavy read-only pipelines where retrying is inherently safe and the cost of silent failure is wasted compute
Retry logic is less appropriate for synchronous user-facing flows where users expect immediate feedback, or for POST/PATCH operations that are not idempotent and do not support idempotency keys.
Follow these steps to add production-ready error handling and retry logic to any API client:
and the network exception types explicitly.
that inspects both HTTP status errors and network timeout exceptions.
header on 429 responses and use its numeric value (seconds) as the minimum sleep time.
— only retry POST/PATCH when the server supports
and you send a stable UUID per logical operation.
Add a circuit breaker for persistent failures
— after a threshold of consecutive failures, stop calling the upstream and fail fast so it can recover.
library provides decorator-based retry logic with composable
predicates. The example below classifies HTTP status errors and network timeouts, inspects the
header, and logs each retry attempt at WARNING level using tenacity's built-in hook.
Retrying is correct for transient failures. But if an upstream service is down for minutes, retrying every call burns resources and blocks callers. A
adds a state machine on top of retry logic:
(upstream declared down — calls fail fast without touching the network), and
library implements this as a decorator:

(treated identically to 503) when the API is temporarily over capacity. The official Python SDK retries automatically with exponential backoff. When calling the API directly in a lightweight agentic harness, include 529 in

is missing or revoked. Retrying wastes quota and always fails. The non-retryable branch raises immediately with the structured body so the operator sees exactly what to fix. A typical 401 body looks like:
— actionable for the operator fixing the credentials and safe to pass to the agent as its error surface. The
is logged separately for support escalation rather than surfaced to the LLM.
— retrying 400/401/403/404/422 wastes time and masks bugs; only 5xx and 429 are appropriate candidates.
— causes duplicate records, double charges, or duplicate notifications when the first attempt succeeded but the response was lost.
— deterministic delays synchronise all clients to retry at the same moment, creating a thundering herd that extends the outage.
— unbounded retries hide bugs and consume resources; always cap at 4–6 attempts.
— retrying before the server's specified window burns requests that count against the same rate limit quota.
— discarding the JSON body loses the request ID and field-level details that operators need for debugging.
Use tenacity for all retry logic
— it handles stop, wait, jitter, logging, and reraise correctly; do not hand-roll loops that miss edge cases.
predicate rather than duplicating if-status logic across every call site.
— prevent exponential growth from parking requests for minutes; the cap prevents worst-case tail latency.
so retry behaviour is visible in production logs during incident diagnosis.
— give the LLM the actionable message only; log raw stack traces, internal paths, and infrastructure details separately.
Add circuit breakers for sustained outages
— after several minutes of 5xx responses, fail fast; let the agent surface the outage rather than spinning for the maximum retry budget on every call.

Retry strategies interact directly with the load profile of the upstream service. At low concurrency, even constant-delay retries are harmless. At high concurrency — many agent workers all hitting the same endpoint — the choice of jitter strategy determines whether a partial outage becomes a full one.
— spreading N concurrent retries uniformly across the backoff window reduces the peak retry rate from a spike of N to approximately N/cap requests per second.
Circuit breakers reduce wasted calls at scale
— 100 agent tasks sharing one downed upstream generate at most a handful of probe requests per reset window instead of 100 per second.
Async retries are essential for agents
during backoff yields the event loop so other tasks continue; a synchronous
Timeouts are mandatory on every call
— without a connect and read timeout, a stalled upstream holds connections open indefinitely, eventually exhausting the connection pool.
Python has a mature ecosystem for HTTP resilience. The main packages to know:
— the standard library for Python retry logic; composable predicates for stop, wait, and retry conditions; fully async-native.
— async-first HTTP client with structured exception types (HTTPStatusError, ConnectTimeout, ReadTimeout) that map cleanly to retry predicates.
— lightweight circuit breaker for Python; decorator API; configurable
— a newer opinionated wrapper around tenacity with built-in instrumentation hooks and a cleaner API for the common case.
Four retry strategies are in common use. The table compares their concrete wait times, jitter behaviour, thundering-herd safety, and ideal use case:
Idempotent reads under very low concurrency; never for writes
Low-traffic background jobs with a single client
Single-client scripts; still unsafe at scale
All production multi-client systems; the only safe default
The exponential + jitter row is the only thundering-herd-safe strategy. At any realistic concurrency level it should be the default choice.
Error handling code has a security surface as well as a reliability one. Three rules to follow:
Never forward raw error bodies to end users or LLM prompts
— bodies may contain stack traces, database connection strings, or internal paths that expose infrastructure details. Extract only the message field.
Treat 401 as a credential rotation signal
— some systems support automatic token refresh on 401; implement this as a one-time refresh attempt rather than a generic retry loop.
Use idempotency keys for POST retries
— generate a UUID once per logical operation and send it as
on every attempt so the server can deduplicate without creating side effects.

Think of HTTP errors the same way an emergency room triages patients. Some conditions require immediate redirection — the patient filled out the wrong form and must fix it before being seen again (4xx: caller mistake, no retry). Some require observation and re-examination after a wait — the doctor is temporarily overloaded but will be free shortly (5xx: transient server fault, retry with backoff). Some require routing to a different hospital entirely because the whole department is overwhelmed (circuit open: upstream down, fail fast and route elsewhere).

The jitter on backoff corresponds to patients arriving at random times across the morning rather than all showing up at 9:00 am: the same total load, but spread to prevent the waiting-room crush that delays everyone. The circuit breaker corresponds to the hospital's diversion policy: when the ER is overwhelmed, ambulances are redirected so the ER has space to recover.
4xx errors are the caller's fault and must never be retried without first fixing the request; 5xx and network errors are server-side transient faults that are safe to retry for idempotent operations.
; without jitter it causes thundering herds; full jitter (random in [0, cap]) spreads retries safely across concurrent clients.
is the standard approach; prefer it over hand-rolled loops.
Circuit breakers (pybreaker) complement retries by stopping all calls to a persistently-down upstream, preventing wasted compute and giving the service time to recover.
Always parse the error body to extract the human-readable message and request_id; give agents a sanitised message and log the full body separately for operators.
header and server support; retrying a non-idempotent POST without it risks duplicate writes, double charges, or duplicate notifications.
